title: Work, Family, Health, and Well‐Being ‐ Edited by Suzanne M. Bianchi, Lynne M. Casper, & Rosalind Berkowitz King (Eds.)
abstract: Work, Family, Health, and Well-Being. Suzanne M. Bianchi, Lynne M. Casper, & Rosalind Berkowitz King (Eds.) Mahwah, NJ: Lawrence Erlbaum Associates. 2005. 583 pp. ISBN 0-8058-5254-9. $145 (cloth). What a mighty tome this is! This edited volume contains dozens of chapters, brought together under the heading of a “Workforce/Workplace Mismatch” in today’s society. The impetus for this book was an interdisciplinary conference with this title, held in June of 2003. Mismatch is a useful concept, I believe, as it puts much-needed attention on workplaces—how they are organized, their demands on workers—in addition to the various needs of workers in today’s economy (as opposed to “work-family balance” which suggests that people should “balance” themselves). Here, the editors attempt to show the various causes and consequences of this mismatch as viewed from many different perspectives. Their goals of interdisciplinarity and complexity are admirable but also lofty. Though the book is certainly inclusive, it has a disjointed quality and would benefit from being pared down and more focused on the original mismatch concept. The editors begin the book with an introduction to the ideas and chapters that follow. They discuss the focus of each of the seven upcoming “parts” and then briefly summarize each chapter within that part. This takes the place of an introduction to each section of the book as it appears. Consequently, I often had to go back to their original introduction, as a way to contextualize each chapter and section in the larger scheme of the book. Let us now take a closer look at each substantive section of the book. Part I, called “Time, Work, and Family,” contains four chapters devoted to time allocation research. These range from a nice summary of this research, to a chapter focused on the increasing prevalence of nonstandard work hours, to a study of how people think and feel about their allocation of time. Interesting findings, here, include that total workloads (considering paid and unpaid labor) are similar for men and women but that mothers “protect” their time with children more than fathers do. Though family routines and rituals have positive effects for children, they are becoming increasingly difficult to achieve. Also, with the increase in nonstandard work hours, the notion of “day” care may become a misnomer. Part II, entitled “The ‘Work’ in Work and Family,” has six chapters devoted to workplaces. These include a chapter considering the factors that influence employer hiring and benefits choices, reviews of work-family policies, and research on such policies; a chapter on the importance of a supportive workplace culture; and one articulating the interdependency of workplaces and communities. Clearly, even with the major changes in the demographics of workers, employers still tend to see supportive policies as extras rather than necessities. The notion of a workplace culture helps explain why workers en masse do not take advantage of the so-called “family friendly” policies. The more supportive of the policies that workers perceive their managers and coworkers to be, the more likely they are to use the benefits available to them. Each of the chapters in Part III, “Disciplinary Perspectives in the Study of Work and Family,” looks at work-family issues from the point of view of a different scholarly discipline: psychology, economics, anthropology, sociology, or public policy. The authors discuss the different subfields of their disciplines as well as the major theoretical perspectives that have been used in work-family research. Although this part of the book is certainly informative, it has too much of a textbook quality to it. The chapters showing research conducted using the different perspectives, found throughout the book, are more useful. In each chapter of Part IV, the authors use a single disciplinary lens (i.e., psychological, economic) to review the existing literature on maternal employment and children’s well-being. The evidence is complex. Particularly for employment during children’s infancy, there appear to be negative effects, but these are more apparent for married families and White non-Hispanic families, and they of course depend on the number and timing of hours worked. The evidence for adolescents is less conclusive, and the authors call for more research in this area. The authors primarily focus here on methodological issues in this research, such as selection effects, the importance of mediating and moderator variables, and the conceptualization of “well-being.” Altogether, the authors are convincing in their argument that the relationship between mothers’ employment and child outcomes is complicated and deserving of methodological sophistication. Out of place here, however, is a chapter offering decidedly advanced quantitative analysis solutions to methodological problems. Oddly, the public policy chapter devotes no real attention to the outcomes for children of maternal employment. The four chapters in Part V, “Gender and Employment, Caregiving and Health” are unified in their focus on gender as an organizing principle in work and family life. One chapter traces the historical development of the concept of “separate spheres,” calling for us to focus on “work-family integration” rather than balance, and argues convincingly that we should redefine what we consider “good” work and commitment to our jobs (in place of outmoded proxies like face time). There is also a nice review of the division of household labor research, followed by a chapter in which the authors discuss the research showing that (happily) married people—especially men—are physically and emotionally healthier than single ones. This section of the book ends with a piece on women in midlife, which is a much-needed reminder that work-family tension is not limited to families with small children but affects workers (particularly women) throughout the life course. I find Part VI to be out of place with the rest of the book. The chapters are supposedly tied together because of connections between workplaces and health. But although the first two chapters (a review of the literature on how occupational injuries and illnesses can affect workers’ families and children, and a chapter showing connections between employment characteristics, social status, and health) do so, the final chapter in this section focuses on a Danish-designed and tested questionnaire on workplace factors that influence the degree to which workers feel conflicted between work and family. Even the chapters on work and health are not directly about families. The final part of the book, Part VII, brings together research focused on lower income families: their work, caregiving, and well-being. The first chapter in this section is on working class families—an often neglected group—and the unique work and family challenges they face. The next is a detailed account of low-skilled jobs, which the authors argue offer increasingly “nonstandard” aspects in terms of job schedules and unclear employment statuses, forcing workers with children to arrange precarious informal care. The authors of the next chapter offer ethnographic evidence showing the sheer prevalence of physical and mental health problems in low-income families, arguing for greater health care access and workplace accommodations. The final chapter takes the work-family issue global, documenting common findings across nations in terms of the effects of work conditions, and the shortage of adequate policy protections, on families. In the conclusion chapter, the editors extol the virtues of using multiple disciplines in work-family research and offer a seeming justification for the sheer length of this volume by declaring that each included area is necessary for understanding the complexity of the issue. Beyond that, the chapter is a useful summary of what good work-family research takes into account—such as context (in its many manifestations), family diversity, causality, and the fact that workplace and government policies are not likely to change without more convincing and accessible research showing their necessity. For whom is this book most suited? If you are teaching a graduate-level seminar on work and family in the social sciences, this could certainly serve as a good supplement. Though the book will likely not be useful in its entirety, it gives a nice overview of the many fascinating areas of research focused on the connections between workplaces and family life in contemporary America.
